relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The council leads Jesus to Pilate.
Accusation reframes him as political danger.
Pilate asks the kingship question; Jesus answers, You say it.
Pilate finds no cause.
The accusers insist he stirs the people.
Galilee becomes the jurisdictional pivot.
Jesus is sent to Herod.
Herod wants spectacle and sign.
Many questions meet Jesus’ silence.
The accusations continue fiercely.
Herod mocks and sends him back.
Opposing rulers become friends through shared contempt.
Pilate gathers leaders and people.
He declares no cause in the charges.
Herod too found nothing worthy of death.
Pilate proposes discipline and release.
A later manuscript notes feast-release custom.
The crowd asks for Barabbas instead.
Barabbas is linked with uprising and murder.
Pilate still wants to release Jesus.
The cry becomes crucifixion.
A third declaration of no death-cause is made.
Their loud voices prevail.
Pilate yields decision to demand.
Barabbas is released and Jesus handed over.
Simon of Cyrene carries the cross behind Jesus.
Women follow with mourning and lament.
Jesus turns to the daughters of Jerusalem.
Coming days will invert even womb-blessing.
The mountains and hills become images of desperate covering.
Green wood warns what dry wood will meet.
Two others are led with him.
At Skull, he is crucified between right and left.
A textual tradition speaks Father-release over those who do not know; garments are divided.
Rulers mock the saving one.
Soldiers join the mockery with sour wine.
They demand self-saving from the king.
The inscription names him king.
One criminal repeats the mockery.
The other rebukes him with fear of the Divine.
He recognizes justice and Jesus’ innocence.
He asks to be remembered in the reign.
Today, Jesus promises presence in Paradise.
Darkness covers the land.
Sun fails and the temple curtain tears.
Jesus entrusts his spirit into the Father’s hands.
The centurion glorifies the Divine and calls him righteous.
The crowd returns beating chests.
The women from Galilee stand as witnesses from afar.
Joseph is good and righteous.
He waits for the Divine reign and did not consent to the action.
He asks Pilate for Jesus’ body.
The body is wrapped in linen and placed in a new stone-cut tomb.
Preparation gives way to Sabbath dawning.
The women see the tomb and placement of the body.
They prepare spices, then rest according to command.
